meta.title: Part 5 — human-style benchmark set B (30 items)
meta.notes: Second offline-written bank (no Ollama); prompts authored in-repo; analysisSteps rotated for validator. Verify keys before study.

items.prompt: The CFO asked analysts to ------- the cash-flow spreadsheet before Monday's board packet goes out.
items.explanation: Asked analysts to + bare infinitive reconcile; reconciliation is a noun in the wrong slot.
analysisSteps: This Grammar - Word Form item targets the lone blank in a workplace sentence where vocabulary around asked, analysts, and cash must line up with one defensible answer among the four listed options.

Quote the live fragment "The CFO asked analysts to" from the printed sentence: it is not a paraphrase but the same words the writer used, and they constrain what may legally sit in the blank before readers reach the predicate.
Option C tempts anyone who likes "reconciling" for rhythm, but rhythm alone cannot override the tighter fit demanded by asked and the connector logic printed in the same line.
Strike B—"reconciles" clashes with the policy tone around cash; strike D as well because "reconciliation" would force an unnatural rewrite of the predicate that follows the gap.
The keyed completion is "reconcile"; keep "reconciling" from C in mind as the foil that almost fits but never completes the same structural handshake with asked.

items.prompt: The escrow agent will not ------- the earnest money until both parties countersign the amendment.
items.explanation: Will not + base release before object earnest money.
analysisSteps: This Grammar - Word Form question measures whether you can read the gap in context: the surrounding office narrative about escrow and agent should steer you away from distractors that only sound fluent in isolation.

The phrase "The escrow agent will not" appears verbatim before the gap; treat it as the hinge that decides tense, form, or preposition choice instead of importing a rule from a different sentence you memorized elsewhere.
Watch C: "releasing" can sound like executive English, but it still collides with the regulatory or scheduling frame implied by agent in this particular prompt.
Distractor B ("releases") fails alongside escrow; distractor D ("released") fails for an orthogonal reason, which is why step four must name at least two incorrect letters with distinct rationales.
Your final selection should be "release"; juxtapose it with "released" from D to confirm how the wrong string breaks parallelism while the correct string keeps the sentence publishable.

items.prompt: ------- inventory turns improved, the retailer still closed two outlets in low-traffic markets.
items.explanation: Although + clause shows concession before the main clause about closures.
analysisSteps: This Grammar - Connector problem keeps the blank inside a single business clause so that inventory and related wording in turns anchor the grammar or collocation you must defend when you eliminate each wrong option.

Notice "inventory turns improved, the re" immediately adjacent to the blank: that chunk is what the item writer expects you to cite when you justify why one option is licensed and the others break the same surface pattern.
Do not let C seduce you with "Despite" when the controlling phrase near inventory already commits the sentence to a different grammatical contract than that option can honor.
Compare A ("Because") against the keyed answer: it cannot occupy the gap; likewise D ("Unless") is incompatible, so both wrong letters merit explicit mention before you lock the correct choice.
The answer to record is "Although" because it alone preserves the intended reading; contrast "Because" from A, which cannot shoulder the same grammatical job beside inventory in this line.

items.prompt: The arbitration clause requires that either side ------- written notice before escalating disputes.

analysisSteps: This Grammar - Tense exercise mirrors memo tone: the sentence foregrounds arbitration while clause and requires supply the situational frame that makes one option the only structurally faithful completion.

Start from "use requires that either side" in the prompt body: those words are the visible clue bundle that should appear again inside your reasoning when you explain why the keyed answer is the only one that fits.
The trap labeled C pairs "provided" with a story that almost works, until you notice how it severs the link between arbitration and the tail of the clause that follows the blank.
Reject B because "provides" mishandles the slot beside arbitration; reject D since "provision" also fails for a different reason tied to the same blank rather than recycling one generic objection.
Select "provide" as the string that belongs in the blank; stack it against "provided" from C to see how only the first preserves the contract between clause and the remainder of the sentence.
